publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.head: Origins and History to 1933
div.p: The Leipzig Conservatory was founded as the Conservatorium der Musik in 1843 at the exhortation of Felix Mendelssohn, who served as its first Rector from then to his death in 1847. It was the first institution of its kind. The Conservatory was initially housed within the Gewandhaus building. Subsequent rectors were Heinrich Conrad Schleinitz (1849–81); Otto Günther (1881–97), during whose time the institution moved into a specially designed new building at Grassistraße 8 and the name was changed to Königliches Konservatorium [Royal Conservatory] der Musik zu Leipzig; Carl Reinecke (1897–1902); Arthur Nikisch (1902–07); Stephan Krehl (1907–24), in which latter year it changed to Landeskonservatorium [State Conservatory] der Musik zu Leipzig; Max Pauer (1924–32); Walther Davisson (1932–42); and Johann Nepomuk David (1942–45).

Reinhard Oppel joined the faculty of the Conservatory in October 1928. As a conservative-minded musician he found the enduring influence of Max Reger, who had been the professor of composition at Leipzig University from 1907 to his death in 1916, upon Conservatory students distasteful, and he was in constant dispute with the powerful professor of organ studies, Karl Straube, speaking of “my struggle against the Straube windmill” and “the Straube clique” (OJ 13/10, [175] and [177]) and battles against successive rectors Max Pauer and Walter Davisson.

div.head: The Third Reich and after
div.p: In 1933, Oppel was involved with a committee of the Nazi Party “to study the prehistory of our institution since its founding by Mendelssohn in 1843.” During the National-Socialist period, Mendelssohn’s connection to the Conservatory was all but obliterated. A fellowship created in his name by the Mendelssohn family, who were Jewish, was discontinued in 1934. Two years later, statues of Mendelssohn in Leipzig (and elsewhere in Germany) were removed. The Conservatory itself was closed in 1944–45.
Today, after amalgamation in 1992, the Conservatory is named the Hochschule für Musik und Theater Felix Mendelssohn Bartholdy Leipzig.
